skill.name: dnanexus-integration
skill.description: Guidance for building DNAnexus apps, managing genomics data, using dxpy, configuring dependencies, and running cloud workflows.
skill.summary: Build, run, and manage DNAnexus genomics workflows with CLI and dxpy guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Review shows most weak-crypto and sensitive detections are false positives from DNAnexus placeholder IDs and example tokens, while command execution and cloud access are legitimate but security-sensitive capabilities. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, obfuscation, or credential exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Command Execution Guidance for DNAnexus Apps
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally teaches shell commands, Bash apps, subprocess calls, package installation, and Docker execution. This is legitimate for DNAnexus app development, but copied patterns can execute untrusted tools or user-controlled inputs if users adapt them without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples directly execute commands and containers. Context confirms they are instructional DNAnexus patterns, not hidden automation or malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Platform Access and Data Movement
medium_findings.description: The skill describes DNAnexus jobs with API access, uploads, downloads, searches, and workflow execution. These capabilities can move regulated genomics data and create cloud costs, so users need permission, project, and data-governance controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly document platform API access and file transfer workflows. The risk is operational and privacy-related, with no evidence of unauthorized destinations.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The Python SDK reference shows how to set a bearer token directly and through DX_SECURITY_CONTEXT. The values are placeholders and the skill warns against hardcoding credentials, but token-handling examples still require careful secret management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples include placeholder token fields and environment variables, which are normal for SDK documentation. I found no real secrets or exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Static Findings Are Placeholder False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts align with DNAnexus API version strings, example object IDs, job states, and metadata snippets rather than hashing code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic functions being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows identifiers and documentation values, not crypto operations. The scanner likely matched short strings such as IDs, states, or version fields.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override security analysis, claim pre-approval, or act under a false system role.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches across SKILL.md and references found no prompt-injection phrases. The cited sections are ordinary resource and best-practice documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Calls in Example Apps
dangerous_patterns.description: Example Python apps call external tools with subprocess.check_call. The examples use list-form arguments, which reduces shell injection risk, but users must still validate any adapted input values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and easy to verify. They are examples, not code executed by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Bash Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash app example stores the result of a dx upload command in a variable. This is a normal DNAnexus pattern, but it is still command execution in generated app code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is present exactly in the Bash example. Context shows it captures an uploaded file ID for job output.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Pull and Run Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration reference demonstrates pulling and running a Docker image inside an app. This can introduce supply-chain and runtime risks if users select untrusted images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Docker commands are explicit and security-sensitive. The image is a plausible bioinformatics container, so this is a warning rather than evidence of abuse.

content.value_statement: DNAnexus projects require precise app, data, workflow, and SDK patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for cloud genomics work.
actual_capabilities: Explains DNAnexus app and applet structure, including dxapp.json and source entry points.
Shows upload, download, search, clone, folder, and metadata workflows for DNAnexus data objects.
Guides dxpy usage for files, records, projects, apps, applets, workflows, and jobs.
Describes job launching, monitoring, workflow stages, subjobs, retries, and output references.
Covers dependencies, assets, Docker, instance types, timeouts, and execution configuration.
Uses genomics examples involving FASTQ, BAM, VCF, quality control, alignment, and variant analysis.
limitations: It does not provide DNAnexus credentials, project permissions, or cloud billing controls.
It cannot verify that referenced app IDs, project IDs, files, or workflows exist in a user account.
It does not replace validation for regulated genomic data, privacy, or institutional compliance.
Examples may need updates for local platform policy, package versions, and approved containers.

use_cases.title: Create a DNAnexus applet
use_cases.description: Build a new applet with inputs, outputs, dxapp.json metadata, and a Python or Bash entry point.

use_cases.title: Automate project data operations
use_cases.description: Search, upload, download, organize, and annotate project files or records with dxpy patterns.

use_cases.title: Run and monitor genomics workflows
use_cases.description: Launch analyses, chain outputs between stages, inspect job status, and handle failed runs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simple Applet
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new DNAnexus executable.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a DNAnexus applet for a single FASTQ input and one report output. Include inputs, outputs, and file layout.

prompt_templates.title: Write dxpy Data Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dxpy plan to find BAM files by project, tag them with sample metadata, and download selected results safely.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Failed Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this DNAnexus job failure summary and suggest checks for inputs, permissions, logs, dependencies, and resource settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Step Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a DNAnexus workflow for quality control, alignment, and variant calling. Include stage inputs, output links, parallelization, and resource guidance.

output_examples.input: Plan a DNAnexus applet that filters FASTQ reads and returns a filtered FASTQ file.
output: Recommended app structure with dxapp.json, source file, resources, and test inputs.
Input and output specification guidance for linked DNAnexus file objects.
Execution notes for downloading the input, running the filter tool, uploading output, and returning a file link.

output_examples.input: Help me organize all BAM outputs from an experiment in one DNAnexus project folder.
output: A step-by-step dxpy workflow for finding matching files, creating folders, and applying metadata.
Permission checks for source and destination projects.
Safety notes about validating file names and avoiding accidental overwrites.

output_examples.input: Explain how to monitor a long-running workflow and recover from a failed stage.
output: Job and analysis state checks for active, completed, failed, and terminated runs.
Log review and failure-message triage steps.
Retry guidance using original inputs and corrected resource or dependency settings.

best_practices: Use least-privilege DNAnexus projects, tokens, and workspace permissions for every workflow.
Pin dependency versions and use approved Docker images or DNAnexus assets for reproducible runs.
Validate input links, file states, project IDs, and output paths before launching jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode DNAnexus API tokens or security contexts in shared source files.
Do not pass untrusted sample names, filenames, or parameters into shell commands without validation.
Do not run large workflows before testing applets with small representative data.

faq.question: Does this skill run DNAnexus jobs by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. A user or agent must execute commands with valid DNAnexus access.

faq.question: Can it help with dxpy scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for files, records, projects, apps, applets, workflows, jobs, and direct API calls.

faq.question: Is it suitable for regulated genomic data?
faq.answer: It can support regulated workflows, but users must apply institutional privacy, consent, retention, and audit requirements.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill covers command execution, Docker, cloud jobs, file transfer, and tokens. These are legitimate but sensitive operations.

faq.question: Does it include real credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence found of real credentials. Token examples use placeholders and include guidance not to hardcode secrets.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
